findings.title: Potential compute exhaustion via iterative exhaustive scanning guidance
findings.description: The SKILL.md instructs the agent to perform repeated full-codebase scans, synthetic request generation, import-chain analysis, git-history cross-referencing, and to continue until convergence is reached. This creates an availability risk because the workflow encourages high-cost repeated processing across the repository and discourages interruption, which may consume excessive CPU/time on large codebases.
findings.snippet: "Exhaustive validation may require multiple passes. The tester will continue scanning until convergence is reached (no new schemas discovered in consecutive passes). This iterative process is essential for accuracy and should not be interrupted." and configuration example: "max_passes: 20"
findings.remediation: Add explicit safety bounds and stop conditions, such as conservative default pass limits, file-count/size caps, timeout budgets, and user confirmation before exhaustive mode. Prefer quick/standard modes by default and require an explicit opt-in for deep repeated scans.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The SKILL.md explicitly recommends exhaustive repeated rescanning, synthetic request generation, import-chain analysis, git-history cross-referencing, and continuing until convergence, which can materially increase compute usage on large repositories.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Easy to trigger by running the documented exhaustive workflow against a large or complex codebase.
metadata.meta_impact: Can waste CPU time, increase latency, and degrade availability or agent responsiveness, especially in constrained environments.

scan_metadata.llm_overall_assessment: The skill does not show prompt injection, direct data exfiltration, credential theft, or obvious command/code injection based on the provided content. The primary concern is availability risk from repeated exhaustive scanning guidance that could become expensive on large repositories. Secondary issues are packaging/manifest hygiene: a missing referenced file and lack of explicit allowed-tools metadata.

meta_correlations.relationship: These findings all concern missing metadata or packaging transparency rather than active exploitation behavior.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Add a proper YAML frontmatter manifest to SKILL.md including at least name, description, license, compatibility, and allowed-tools; also ensure any referenced files are actually bundled or remove the references.

meta_recommendations.title: Add hard execution limits to exhaustive validation
meta_recommendations.fix: Revise SKILL.md to require bounded execution, e.g. set a strict max_passes, max_runtime_minutes, and max_files_scanned; instruct the agent to stop after the first materially complete pass unless the user explicitly approves deeper analysis. Example: 'For exhaustive mode, stop after max_passes: 3 or 10 minutes, whichever comes first, and ask for confirmation before rescanning the full repository.'

meta_recommendations.title: Declare tool boundaries explicitly
meta_recommendations.fix: Add YAML frontmatter to SKILL.md with an explicit allowed-tools list, for example: 'allowed-tools: [Read, Bash, Python]'. This makes intended execution boundaries auditable.

meta_recommendations.title: Add missing manifest metadata
meta_recommendations.fix: Add a license field in the manifest frontmatter, for example: 'license: MIT' or the project's actual license.

meta_recommendations.title: Remove or package unresolved file references
meta_recommendations.fix: If 'both.py' is required, include it in the skill package and document its role. If it is not required, remove the reference so the package contents match the documentation.

meta_risk_assessment.summary: The skill is mostly benign; the only substantive security concern is guidance that encourages potentially expensive repeated analysis passes on large codebases.
meta_risk_assessment.top_priority: Bound the exhaustive scanning workflow with hard limits on passes, time, and repository scope.
meta_risk_assessment.verdict_reasoning: There is no evidence of exfiltration, prompt injection, or system compromise. However, the instructions explicitly encourage repeated full-repository rescans until convergence and discourage interruption, which creates a mild availability/resource-abuse risk.
